options: Lower latency on every query regardless of access pattern
Very high write throughput when access is by a known primary key
Richer multi-table JOINs and ad-hoc query support
Stronger out-of-the-box consistency than a single-node SQL DB
why: The real differentiator is the access pattern plus write profile, not ACID. NoSQL shines for huge write throughput and queries by a known key; SQL wins for complex ad-hoc queries, joins, and transactions.
more: Cassandra and DynamoDB are tuned for partition-key access; ask the wrong question (joins, ranges across keys) and they get awkward or slow.

options: NoSQL, because horizontal write scaling outweighs other concerns
SQL, because multi-row transactions, integrity, and ad-hoc queries matter even at the cost of scale
NoSQL, modeling balances as a key-value map with atomic counters
SQL or NoSQL, since at sufficient scale the trade-offs even out
why: Finance and billing are the classic 'SQL despite scale' case: transactions, joins, and auditability matter more than raw write throughput. NoSQL fits activity feeds, telemetry, and chat, not ledgers.
more: You can scale SQL for ledgers via partitioning and read replicas; you cannot easily bolt multi-row ACID transactions onto an eventually-consistent store.

options: Activity / news feed
Double-entry accounting ledger
Device telemetry and time-series data
Chat message history
why: Feeds, telemetry, and chat are high-volume, key-accessed, and tolerant of eventual consistency. Double-entry accounting needs multi-row transactions, so it belongs in SQL.

options: Making cross-node reads strongly consistent by default
Horizontal scaling of data and load beyond one node's capacity
Removing the need for replication and failover
Automatically detecting and rebalancing hot keys
why: Sharding is horizontal partitioning to scale past a single node. It does not remove the need for replication, and a bad shard key can create hotspots rather than eliminate them.

options: Hot shards from a celebrity or otherwise skewed key
Cross-shard queries and distributed transactions
The operational cost of resharding and rebalancing
Losing the ability to build indexes on the data
why: The three big pains are hotspots, cross-shard joins and transactions, and the cost of resharding. Indexes still exist; they are just maintained per shard rather than globally.

options: Convert the entire dataset to range sharding instead of hashing
Isolate or split the hot key with sub-sharding, dedicated handling, or a cache
Raise the replication factor across every shard in the cluster
Drop sharding and consolidate back onto a single large node
why: Hotspots are handled by isolating or splitting the hot key (dedicated shard, key-splitting, caching the celebrity), not by re-architecting the whole scheme.

options: A standby replica is promoted to accept writes only after failover
Multiple nodes accept writes simultaneously, often one per data center
One node exclusively serves reads while another exclusively serves writes
Clients send each write to a quorum of replicas with no fixed leader
why: Multi-leader means several nodes accept writes at once, common across data centers; its core pain is write-conflict resolution. A standby promoted on failover describes single-leader.

options: By electing a dedicated leader for each partition
By tuning quorums so that writes plus reads exceed replicas (W + R > N)
By coordinating writes through two-phase commit
By reading from a single replica to avoid conflicts
why: Leaderless systems have clients write and read multiple replicas; you get strong-ish consistency when the write quorum plus the read quorum exceed the replica count (W + R > N).

options: The system blocks reads until a write has fully replicated everywhere
A user is guaranteed to see their own just-completed write
All users observe a given write at the same instant
Every read is routed to the leader to avoid stale data
why: Read-your-writes means a user sees their own just-made write even if other replicas lag. It does not imply global synchronization or that everyone sees the write instantly.

options: By resolving write conflicts between the competing leaders
By promoting a replica to leader through failover and election
By satisfying reads from a quorum until a leader returns
By resharding the data across the surviving nodes
why: In single-leader replication all writes go to one node; on a crash a replica is promoted to leader via failover and leader election.

options: Cache, Availability, Performance
Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
Consistency, Atomicity, Persistence
Concurrency, Availability, Parallelism
why: CAP stands for Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. It is easy to blank on the A and the P under pressure, so anchor them.

options: Consistency versus Partition tolerance in steady state
Availability versus Partition tolerance during normal operation
Consistency versus Availability while a partition is happening
Read latency versus Consistency in the common case
why: Partitions are inevitable, so you cannot trade away P. The actual decision is C versus A when a partition occurs: a CP system refuses to answer, an AP system answers but may be stale.
accept: Option 3 (latency vs consistency) is the real everyday trade-off, but that is the PACELC 'Else' case, not the CAP question being asked here.

options: A third consistency tier between strong and eventual
That even with no partition you still trade Latency versus Consistency
A mechanism to prevent network partitions from occurring
A standard protocol for detecting partitions quickly
why: PACELC says if there is a Partition choose A or C, Else (normal operation) choose Latency or Consistency. It captures the everyday latency cost of strong consistency that CAP ignores.

options: The database pushes the value into the cache after the query
The application code populates the cache after reading from the DB
The cache fetches and loads the value from the DB itself
A background warming job is the only thing that fills it
why: In cache-aside (lazy loading) the app checks the cache, and on a miss it reads the DB and then populates the cache. Write-through writes cache and DB synchronously; write-back defers the DB write.

options: Cache stampede, also called the thundering herd
Cache-aside lazy loading working as designed
A hot shard caused by a skewed partition key
Write amplification from repeated cache updates
why: This is a cache stampede or thundering herd. Mitigate it with a lock or single-flight so only one request rebuilds, TTL jitter, or refresh-ahead.

options: A lock or single-flight so only one request rebuilds the key
TTL jitter so popular keys do not all expire at the same moment
Refresh-ahead that rebuilds the value just before it expires
Bypassing the cache entirely while the system is under load
why: A lock or single-flight, TTL jitter, and refresh-ahead all either spread out or collapse the rebuild. Bypassing the cache just sends every request straight to the DB.

options: Choosing an efficient value compression scheme
Cache stampede, the thundering herd on rebuild
Picking a fast serialization format for entries
Designing a consistent key-naming convention
why: Beyond eviction, invalidation/staleness and cache stampede are the two cache problems that bite hardest in interviews.

options: The raw file bytes stored in a binary BLOB column
Only the object-store key or URL referencing the file
A base64-encoded copy of the file contents
A thumbnail plus the inline binary for fast access
why: Keep binaries in object storage like S3 or GCS and store only the URL or reference in the DB. Stuffing binaries into rows wrecks index and cache efficiency.

options: Run your application's business logic close to the user
Geo-cache static content near users to cut latency and offload origin
Act as the primary datastore for dynamic application data
Encrypt all traffic between the client and the origin server
why: A CDN is a geographically distributed edge cache for static assets that reduces latency and origin load. It often sits in front of blob storage.
accept: Edge compute (option 1) does run logic at the edge, but that is an add-on; geo-caching static content is the CDN's defining job.

options: Multi-threaded execution with one lock acquired per key
Single-threaded command execution, so a heavy command blocks the whole instance
A separate OS process forked for each client connection
Lock-free parallel execution spread across all CPU cores
why: Redis executes commands single-threaded, which gives atomicity for free, but an expensive command such as KEYS or a big SORT stalls everything else.
more: Networking I/O can be threaded in modern Redis, but the command-execution model itself is single-threaded.

options: Consistent but not durable by default
Durable on disk but not internally consistent
Both strongly consistent and durable out of the box
Neither consistent nor durable in practice
why: A single node is consistent (one copy, atomic ops) but not durable by default since it is in-memory. The common miss is saying it sacrifices consistency, when it actually sacrifices durability.

options: A consistent-hashing ring with virtual nodes
16384 fixed hash slots, slot equals CRC16(key) mod 16384
Range partitioning based on the key prefix
Random slot assignment rebalanced periodically
why: Redis Cluster uses 16384 fixed hash slots (CRC16 mod 16384), not a consistent-hashing ring. Slots move between nodes on rebalance, and clients follow MOVED and ASK redirects.

options: allkeys-lru, evicting the least recently used key
volatile-ttl, evicting the key with the nearest expiry
noeviction, so writes return an error once memory is full
allkeys-random, evicting an arbitrary key
why: The default is noeviction: once maxmemory is hit, writes fail rather than silently dropping data. You must opt into an LRU or LFU policy to use Redis as a pure cache.

options: RDB point-in-time snapshots
AOF append-only command log
Single-threaded command execution
MemoryDB for true multi-AZ durability
why: RDB snapshots and the AOF log both add durability (AOF everysec loses about a second, AOF always loses nothing but is slower), and MemoryDB provides true durability. Single-threading is about execution, not durability.

options: Wrap the steps in a server-side Lua script or a MULTI/EXEC block
Run a two-phase commit transaction across the cluster nodes
Rely on each separate command already running single-threaded
Hold a client-side mutex around the sequence of calls
why: A Lua script runs atomically on the server, and MULTI/EXEC with WATCH also works. Separate commands are not atomic relative to each other even though each individual command is.

options: Streams deliver messages with lower latency than Pub/Sub
Pub/Sub is at-most-once with no persistence; Streams persist messages and add consumer groups
Pub/Sub persists messages while Streams are fire-and-forget
They are functionally equivalent with different command names
why: Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget and at-most-once, so offline subscribers miss messages. Streams persist the log and support consumer groups and replay, making them a Kafka-lite.

options: It provides safe mutual exclusion under all failure conditions
Kleppmann showed it is unsafe under GC pauses and clock skew, so use fencing tokens or a consensus system for correctness-critical locks
It only ever functions correctly when run on a single node
It depends on MemoryDB to provide its locking guarantees
why: Redlock works for efficiency locks, but Kleppmann showed it is not safe for correctness under pauses or clock issues. Mention fencing tokens or a consensus store like ZooKeeper or etcd for hard locks.

options: L4 adds latency overhead that makes it slower than L7
L4 routes by IP and port and is content-blind; L7 reads URL, headers, and cookies for content routing, TLS termination, and stickiness
L7 operates purely at the TCP transport layer like L4
L4 can inspect cookies to make sticky-session decisions
why: L4 is transport-layer (TCP/UDP), fast but content-blind. L7 is application-layer and can route by URL, header, or cookie, terminate TLS, and do sticky sessions.

options: Round-robin across the backend pool
Least-connections to the busiest-aware backend
Consistent hashing keyed on the client or request
Two-phase commit across the backends
why: Round-robin, least-connections, weighted, and consistent hashing are all load-balancing strategies. Two-phase commit is a distributed-transaction protocol, not a routing algorithm.

options: Messages are deleted as soon as a consumer acknowledges them
It is an append-only log: messages are retained and replayable while consumers track their own offset
Only a single consumer can ever read from a given topic
Messages arrive in no guaranteed order within a topic
why: Kafka is a distributed append-only log: messages are retained and replayable, consumers track offsets, multiple consumer groups read the same data independently, and order is preserved within a partition.

options: It is retained on the queue for later replay by others
It is deleted from the queue and not redelivered
It is moved to a different partition for archival
It is broadcast to every other registered consumer
why: Broker queues delete a message on ack, typically with one consumer and no replay. They are good for simple background tasks and decoupling, not stream replay or analytics.

options: Decoupling producers from consumers in time and deployment
Smoothing load by buffering bursts of incoming work
Providing strong cross-service transactional consistency
Throttling and protecting slower downstream consumers
why: Queues decouple services, smooth and buffer bursts, and throttle downstream load. They do not provide strong consistency and generally push you toward eventual consistency.

options: SQS, where each message is deleted once one consumer processes it
Kafka with a separate consumer group for each team
A single shared RabbitMQ queue all three teams drain
A database table that each team polls and deletes rows from
why: Kafka's multiple consumer groups each keep their own offset over the same retained log, which is exactly the multi-consumer fan-out case.

options: Assemble each user's timeline on demand at read time
On each post, write the item into every follower's precomputed timeline
Pull a user's followees' posts when they open the app
Cache the rendered home page at the CDN edge
why: Fan-out-on-write (push) fans each post into every follower's feed on write. Reads are fast but writes are expensive, and it breaks down for celebrities with millions of followers.

options: Per-follower read latency becomes unacceptably slow
A single post triggers 100 million timeline writes
Post ordering across followers can no longer be preserved
The resulting timelines become impossible to cache
why: One celebrity post means 100 million writes. That is why the standard answer is a hybrid: push for normal users and pull (fan-out-on-read) for celebrities.

options: Always fan-out-on-write into every follower's feed
Always fan-out-on-read by assembling feeds on request
Hybrid: push for normal users and pull for celebrities
Recompute every timeline from scratch on each request
why: The hybrid balances write cost and read latency: precompute feeds for the many normal users and compute on read for the few huge accounts.

options: It counts requests per fixed window and resets the counter each window
It holds N tokens refilled at a steady rate; each request takes one, and an empty bucket rejects
It delays every request by a fixed interval to pace traffic
It admits requests but drops every Nth one to shed load
why: Token bucket keeps a bucket of N tokens refilled at a steady rate; a request consumes a token and an empty bucket rejects. It allows bursts up to the bucket size.

options: Inside the database, enforced per query
At the edge in the API gateway or load balancer, keyed per user, IP, or API key
In the client application before requests are sent
In the message queue between the services
why: Put the limiter at the edge (gateway or load balancer) so abuse is rejected before reaching downstream services, keyed per user, IP, or API key for fairness and DDoS protection.

options: Lower memory footprint than a simple counter
Smooth handling of short bursts up to the bucket size
Perfect counting accuracy right at the window boundary
Operation without any persistent state at all
why: Token bucket allows controlled bursts and avoids the fixed-window edge problem of double traffic at the boundary. A sliding-window log or counter also fixes that edge issue.

options: It is guaranteed to execute exactly one time, ever
Applying it many times yields the same result as applying it once
It is designed so that it can never return an error
It completes successfully without any network round trip
why: Idempotent means repeating an operation has the same effect as doing it once. This matters because retries are inevitable: a timeout leaves the client unsure whether the call succeeded.

options: Acquire an exclusive lock on the user's account during the charge
Have the client send an idempotency key the server dedupes on
Permit the client to retry the request at most one time
Disable client retries so a charge is only ever sent once
why: The client sends a unique idempotency key; the server records it and returns the original result on retries instead of charging again.

options: Payment and other write APIs that clients may retry
At-least-once queue consumers that must dedupe redeliveries
Pure read-only GET endpoints with no side effects
Any operation traveling over an unreliable network
why: Retryable writes, at-least-once consumers, and anything over flaky networks all need idempotency. Pure GETs are already naturally idempotent and need nothing extra.

options: Isolation, preventing concurrent transactions from interfering
Durability, ensuring committed data survives a crash
Atomicity, so all participants commit or none do across services
Consistency, keeping invariants valid after each transaction
why: It is primarily Atomicity across nodes: all participants commit or none do. A common miss is saying A and I, but Saga in particular deliberately drops isolation.

options: Optimistic and lock-free, settling to eventual consistency
Pessimistic prepare-and-commit phases holding locks, CP and blocking, with a coordinator SPOF
Asynchronous compensation that undoes steps after the fact
A messaging pattern built on a durable broker queue
why: Two-phase commit is pessimistic and coordinator-driven with prepare and commit phases, holds locks, and is CP and blocking; the coordinator is a single point of failure (mitigated but real). It underpins Spanner and XA.
